topic: General patterns in Overstory swarm orchestration system

sources.finding: Project-agnostic swarm system: TypeScript/Bun, 8 agent roles, zero runtime deps, 2000+ tests
Operating model: orchestrator-lead-worker hierarchy, two-layer agent definitions, SQLite WAL, zero deps, propulsion principle
Coordinator is read-only, spawns leads only, monitors via mail, merges on merge_ready signal
Leaf agents: worktree-isolated, file-scoped, propulsion-driven, mandatory worker_done signal
Mid-tier: decomposes tasks, enforces non-overlapping file ownership, signals merge_ready
4-tier merge escalation: clean → auto-resolve → AI-resolve → reimagine
Tier 2 watchdog: adaptive patrol cadence, progressive nudging, pattern detection
Typed system: 8 capabilities, 10 message types, 4 resolution tiers, session checkpoints
9 typed error classes with machine-readable codes and domain-specific context
Self-critique: error compounding, cost amplification, architectural drift, debugging complexity
Prior cnogo research identified 7 borrowable patterns with adopt/skip recommendations

summary: 15 distinct patterns identified across architecture, communication, isolation, testing, and error handling
Core philosophy: prevention over resolution (non-overlapping file scopes prevent most merge conflicts)
Immediate execution (propulsion principle) avoids token-expensive approval loops
Zero runtime dependency constraint independently converged with cnogo's stdlib-only rule
Named failure modes in agent definitions serve as referenceable documentation and monitoring hooks
SQLite mail with typed payloads provides auditable communication history vs ephemeral native messaging
Self-aware risk assessment (STEELMAN.md) acknowledges swarms only justified for truly independent tasks
recommendation: Use as architectural reference for cnogo's agent team evolution. Most impactful patterns to adopt: tiered merge resolution, named failure modes (already started in team.md fix), and colocated testing. Avoid: full SQLite mail (overkill for current scale), reimagine merge tier (cost risk).
